title: Profissões e Verbos
subtitle: Professions & Regular Verbs
description: Learn professions vocabulary, practice regular -ar verb conjugations, and describe yourself in detail.

vocabulary.example: Eu falo inglês e português. Tu falas espanhol?
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I speak English and Portuguese. Do you speak Spanish?
vocabulary.tip: Pattern: fal- + o/as/a/amos/am. All -ar verbs follow this pattern

vocabulary.example: Eu moro em Lisboa. Onde moras tu?
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I live in Lisbon. Where do you live?
vocabulary.tip: mor- + o/as/a/amos/am: moro, moras, mora, moramos, moram
Note the 'lh' sound: trabalh-: trabalho, trabalhas, trabalha...

vocabulary.example: Eu gosto de música. Gostas de desporto?
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I like music. Do you like sports?
vocabulary.tip: 'Gostar' is always followed by 'de': gostar de + noun/infinitive

vocabulary.example: O João é médico. A Ana é médica.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: João is a doctor. Ana is a doctor.
vocabulary.tip: In Portuguese, no article before profession after SER: 'Sou médico' (not 'Sou um médico')
Masculine ends in -or, feminine adds -a: professor → professora

vocabulary.example: O Nori é engenheiro e mora em Londres.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Nori is an engineer and lives in London.

vocabulary.tip: Professions ending in -ista are the same for masculine and feminine
Also same form for both genders — just change the article

vocabulary.example: Sou solteiro. Ela é casada com o Pedro.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I am single. She is married to Pedro.
vocabulary.tip: Marital status adjectives agree in gender: casado (m.) / casada (f.)

vocabulary.example: — Qual é a tua profissão? — Sou estudante.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: — What is your profession? — I am a student.
vocabulary.tip: The most common way to ask about someone's job in Portuguese

sections.heading: How Regular -ar Verbs Work
sections.body: The majority of Portuguese verbs end in -ar, and most of them are regular — meaning they follow a perfectly predictable pattern. To conjugate any regular -ar verb, you remove the -ar ending to get the 'stem', then add the appropriate ending for each person. The endings are: -o (eu), -as (tu), -a (ele/ela), -amos (nós), -am (eles/elas). Once you learn this pattern, you can conjugate hundreds of verbs. The key insight is that the stem never changes — only the ending does.

sections.heading: Regular -ar Conjugation Pattern (using FALAR as model)
Common Regular -ar Verbs
sections.body: Here are the most useful regular -ar verbs for beginners. Notice how they all follow the exact same pattern — only the stem changes. Practice by picking any of these verbs and running through all five persons: stem + o, stem + as, stem + a, stem + amos, stem + am.

sections.heading: The Verb GOSTAR DE
sections.body: GOSTAR DE (to like) is one of the most useful -ar verbs, but it works differently from English. It always requires the preposition 'de' after it: 'gostar de + noun' or 'gostar de + infinitive'. The thing you like comes AFTER 'de'. This is the opposite of English where the object comes directly after the verb. For example: 'Eu gosto de café' (I like coffee), 'Tu gostas de música?' (Do you like music?). Note: in European Portuguese, the 'de' often contracts with articles: 'do' (de + o), 'da' (de + a), 'dos', 'das'.

sections.heading: Professions & Gender Agreement
sections.body: In Portuguese, profession nouns must agree in gender with the person doing the job. There are three main patterns: (1) Verbs ending in -or change to -ora for feminine: professor → professora. (2) Nouns ending in -eiro change to -eira: engenheiro → engenheira. (3) Nouns ending in -ista are the same for both genders — only the article changes: o jornalista / a jornalista. After the verb SER, you do NOT use an indefinite article before a profession: 'Sou médico' (not 'Sou um médico'). However, if you add an adjective, you DO use the article: 'Sou um bom médico'.

items.portuguese: O João é médico. A Ana é médica.
items.translation: João is a doctor. Ana is a doctor.
items.pronunciation: oo ZHOH-ow eh MEH-dee-koo. ah AH-nah eh MEH-dee-kah.

items.portuguese: Ela é engenheira e trabalha em Londres.
items.translation: She is an engineer and works in London.
items.pronunciation: EH-lah eh en-jen-YAY-rah ee trah-BAHL-yah eem LON-dresh.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes & Tips
items: After SER, drop the article before professions: 'Sou estudante' (not 'Sou um estudante'). But with adjectives: 'Sou um bom estudante'.
Don't forget the 'lh' in 'trabalhar' — it's trah-bahl-YAR, not trah-bal-AR. The 'lh' makes a 'ly' sound.
GOSTAR always needs 'de': 'Gosto de café', NOT 'Gosto café'. This is one of the most common beginner mistakes.
The verb 'morar' always uses 'em' for location: 'Moro em Lisboa', NOT 'Moro a Lisboa'.
For professions ending in -ista, the word doesn't change — only the article: 'o jornalista' / 'a jornalista'. Don't try to add -a!
When using -ar verbs, remember that 'nós' always ends in -amos. This is the only person with this ending, so it's a reliable anchor point.
In European Portuguese, the 'l' at the end of infinitives is pronounced like a dark 'l' (similar to the 'l' in English 'full'), not a clear 'l'.

exercises.title: Conjugate the -ar Verb
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct conjugation for each sentence.

items.question: Eu ___ português e inglês. (falar)
items.explanation: Eu + stem + o = falo. First person singular of regular -ar verbs always ends in -o.

items.question: Ela ___ em Lisboa. (morar)
items.explanation: Ela + stem + a = mora. Third person singular of regular -ar verbs always ends in -a.

items.question: Nós ___ muito para os exames. (estudar)
items.explanation: Nós + stem + amos = estudamos. First person plural of regular -ar verbs always ends in -amos.

items.question: Eles ___ num hospital. (trabalhar)
items.explanation: Eles + stem + am = trabalham. Third person plural of regular -ar verbs always ends in -am.

exercises.title: Complete the Verb Conjugation
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank with the correct form of the verb in parentheses.

exercises.title: Build Sentences with -ar Verbs
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct verb form to complete each sentence.

exercises.title: Correct Profession Form
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct gendered form of the profession.

items.question: A Maria é _____ (engineer).
items.explanation: The feminine of 'engenheiro' is 'engenheira' — the pattern is -eiro → -eira.

items.question: O Pedro é _____ (journalist).
items.explanation: Professions ending in -ista are the same for both genders. 'O Pedro é jornalista'.

items.question: A Ana é _____ (teacher).
items.explanation: The feminine of 'professor' is 'professora' — the pattern is -or → -ora.
